types.title: Pick the track that fits your time and capital
types.subtitle: Both tracks earn from onboarding, data entry and hardware. Only the Sales Partner gets a subscription revenue share.

service.description: You handle onboarding, training and client support. Low investment, flexible hours, steady side income.

sales.description: You sell, onboard and support clients. Higher investment and hours — but you earn 50% of subscription revenue in year one.

calculator.stores: Stores you'll sign up in year one
calculator.arpuNote: Calculation: stores × {value} avg ARPU × 50% commission (Sales Partner, year 1).
calculator.disclaimer: Estimates based on Refresh Pro plan ARPU. Actual results vary by upsell, churn and hardware/onboarding add-ons.

q1.q: Do I need a registered company?
q1.a: For the Sales Partner track we require a legal entity (IP / OOO / TOO). For the Service Partner track a personal contract is fine.

q2.a: Commission becomes payable once the client has actively used Refresh for 7 days. From then on, subscription revenue is split with you monthly for the first 12 months.

q3.q: How and when am I paid?
q3.a: Commissions are calculated on the 1st of each month and paid by the 10th. We pay via bank transfer, Payme, Click, Alif or Korti Milli.

q4.q: What if a shop cancels after I sign them up?
q4.a: You earn commission only on months the shop actually pays. No clawback — if the shop cancels later, you keep everything already earned.

q5.q: Do I have to install hardware?
q5.a: No — Refresh runs on the shop's tablet, laptop or phone. But selling hardware (scanners, printers, receipt paper) is one of your additional income streams.

q6.q: Can I charge the shop for onboarding?
q6.a: Yes — partners typically charge $50–100 per turnkey setup, plus $20/day for data entry. You keep 100% of those fees.

seo.title: Investors — Refresh
seo.description: Refresh is building the operating system for 2.4M+ small retailers across Central Asia — a $87B retail market riding 18% cloud-POS growth.

hero.title: Arming 2.4 million small retailers to compete with the giants.
hero.subtitle: Refresh is the operating system for Central Asian retail — POS, inventory, CRM, finance, marketing in one cloud platform. We give a family boutique in Khujand the same toolkit as a supermarket chain.

stats.smbs: SMBs in our four launch markets

mission.title: Small businesses deserve enterprise-grade tools.
mission.body: Large retail chains run on billion-dollar software stacks. The family store next door still counts stock on paper and loses customers to the chain down the street. Refresh closes that gap — one cloud platform that turns a solo shop into a data-driven operation in 15 minutes.

opportunity.title: A $87B retail market, under-served and digitizing fast.
opportunity.subtitle: Central Asia is the last large unautomated retail region — and the fastest-digitizing one.

tam.title: Total retail turnover
tam.description: Combined 2024 retail turnover across Uzbekistan, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan.

sam.title: Small & medium retailers
sam.description: Registered SMBs in our launch markets — the core audience underserved by legacy 1C and enterprise POS.

som.title: Target stores by 2030
som.description: ~1.7% market share at blended ARPU of $50/mo — our explicit 2030 target.

opportunity.sources: Sources: Stat Agency of Uzbekistan (2025), Bureau of National Statistics Kazakhstan (2024), NSC Kyrgyzstan (2025), Trend.az / EBRD Tajikistan (2024–25), DataReportal Digital 2024.

problem.title: Small retailers are losing to scale — and don't know why.
problem.body: A Dushanbe boutique has no way to know what's selling, what's dead stock, or which customer hasn't come back. Big chains do. That information gap is the single biggest driver of SMB closure in the region.

p1.title: Inventory blindness
p1.body: Most SMBs track stock in a paper ledger or a single Excel file — updated weekly at best, impossible to reconcile across locations.

p2.title: No customer memory
p2.body: Repeat buyers are anonymous. No loyalty, no segmentation, no way to reactivate a lapsed customer.

p3.title: Cash-flow by feel
p3.body: Owners know revenue but not margin. 40% of SMB closures cite cash-flow mismanagement.

p4.title: Legacy software is wrong-sized
p4.body: 1C requires an IT contractor and desktop licenses. Global SaaS (Shopify POS, Lightspeed) is English-only and cards-first — a bad fit for cash-heavy CA markets.

quote.label: From the field
quote.text: I was losing around 15% of stock a year to shrinkage and didn't even know it until I saw the first Refresh report.
quote.author: Shop owner interview, Dushanbe 2025

solution.title: One platform, built for how Central Asia actually shops.
solution.subtitle: Four things we do that global SaaS won't.

s1.title: Four languages, day one
s1.body: Tajik, Russian, Uzbek, English — per-employee. No other cloud POS covers this stack natively.

s2.title: Offline-first POS
s2.body: Internet in TJ is still 42% — our register keeps selling when the connection drops and syncs on return.

s3.title: Local payments & tax
s3.body: Integrated with Humo, Alif, Korti Milli, Payme, Click — and compliant with local fiscalization out of the box.

s4.title: Built for cash-heavy retail
s4.body: Cash handling, supplier debts, informal wholesale — modeled natively instead of bolted on.

w1.label: Global cloud-POS CAGR · 2025–2030
w1.body: Cloud POS is replacing desktop legacy globally. Asia Pacific is leading at 19.6% — and CA is under-penetrated vs. regional peers.

w2.label: Internet penetration · KZ / UZ (2024)
w2.body: Digital rails are in place — our core markets now have the connectivity for cloud SaaS to actually work.

w3.label: Proof of exit · June 2025
w3.body: Our closest comp, Billz (4,000+ stores), was acquired by TBC Bank Group in mid-2025 — validating the thesis and pricing the category.

traction.title: A clear path from 500 to 40,000 stores.
traction.subtitle: Our 5-year plan mirrors Billz's trajectory — with a wider regional footprint and a stronger local-language product.

chart.title: Stores on Refresh
chart.subtitle: Plan · paying customers, year-end
chart.callout: 40,000 stores ≈ 1.7% share of a 2.4M SMB market — conservative vs. Billz's 4,000-store exit in a narrower footprint.

roadmap.title: One market per year — and each one funds the next.

r1.title: 🇹🇯 Tajikistan — dominate the home market
r1.body: Concentrated launch in Dushanbe, Khujand and Bokhtar. Alif · DC · Humo · Korti Milli live. Target 500 paying stores and 100 Growth Partnerships. No new market opens until unit economics are proven.

r2.title: 🇺🇿 Uzbekistan — replicate the playbook
r2.body: Tashkent office. UZS multi-currency · Payme · Click · Uzum · LakLak integrations. Tajikistan hits 1,500 stores; Uzbekistan hits 1,000. Series A raised on proven 2-market expansion.

r3.title: 🇰🇿 Kazakhstan — funded by cash flow
r3.body: Almaty + Astana entry. KZT pricing · Kaspi / Halyk integrations · B2B supplier marketplace launches. Total 8,000 stores. Third-market expansion funded by UZ+TJ revenue, not venture capital.

r4.title: 🇰🇬 Kyrgyzstan + embedded finance
r4.body: Bishkek launch. Working-capital loans to Growth Partnership merchants using their Refresh transaction history. This is where we monetize the Billz→TBC playbook on our own terms.

team.title: Jockeys, not just horses.
team.body: Two operators with direct accountability for every dollar of this raise. Shohrukh Jalolov (CEO) previously shipped [specific company × role × outcome — TO FILL]. [Co-founder name] (CTO) previously led [specific product × users × outcome — TO FILL]. Engineering leadership for mobile, web, and backend hired before month 3.

unitEconomics.subtitle: We don't want your money on hope. Here's what we'll have demonstrated in Tajikistan before you wire a dollar — and the exact triggers that tell us to pivot if any metric breaks.

unitEconomics.killTitle: What makes us pivot — not burn your capital
unitEconomics.killSubtitle: We've pre-committed to specific metric thresholds. If any one breaks for two consecutive months, we cut that line and redirect capital.

k1.action: freeze paid acquisition · pivot to pure partner-sales channel

k2.action: restructure revenue-share terms or sunset the program

k3.trigger: If Tajikistan store count < 300 by month 18
k3.action: delay Uzbekistan entry by 12 months · fix the playbook first

unitEconomics.fundTitle: How the $960K returns — explicit math for fintech-literate investors

f4.label: Avg. revenue share recovery per merchant

risks.title: What could kill this company — and what we do about it.
risks.subtitle: Every risk below is priced in. We're not pretending they don't exist — we're showing how we respond when they materialize.

r1.label: Risk 1
r1.title: Geopolitical & currency volatility
r1.risk: Tajikistan borders Afghanistan. Uzbekistan decontrolled currency in 2016, wiping out foreign capital overnight. Kazakhstan had civil unrest in 2022. Kyrgyzstan's government has changed 3 times in 20 years.
r1.mitigant: Revenue invoiced in local currency, banked in USD-pegged accounts where possible. 6-month cash buffer in USD. Legal entity per country with local counsel. Pilot validates one market before capital deploys to next.

r2.label: Risk 2
r2.title: Growth Partnership adverse selection
r2.risk: Merchants who sign up for revenue-share might be the desperate ones, not the good ones. Strong merchants prefer to keep 100% of their upside.
r2.mitigant: Partnership is gated — we qualify merchants on 90 days of subscription use first, proving basic competency. Revenue-share is only on incremental growth above a 3-month trailing baseline, aligning with strong operators who want marketing capital.

r3.label: Risk 3
r3.title: Kaspi / TBC-backed competitor enters with free software
r3.risk: Kaspi.kz offers free POS bundled with payments in Kazakhstan. TBC Bank (owner of Billz) could replicate the Growth Partnership model with banking muscle. Shopify / Square could finally localize.
r3.mitigant: Tajikistan has no Kaspi equivalent. We get 2-year head start before KZ entry. Growth Partnership contracts are 24-month — switching cost compounds. Shopify still can't offer halal-compliant or offline-first for under $5M engineering investment.

r4.label: Risk 4
r4.title: Founder capacity — building + selling + managing a fintech fund
r4.risk: Buffett's real objection: two founders managing product + sales + partnership fund + 4-country expansion + 5 engineers = too many jobs. Burnout risk.
r4.mitigant: Clear role split: CEO owns sales + partnerships + fundraising. CTO owns product + engineering. Head of Operations hired by month 4 (funded from the 14% Ops line). No geographic expansion handled personally by founders — each new market gets a dedicated country lead before entry.

growthModel.title: We don't just sell software. We invest in our customers.
growthModel.subtitle: Most retail platforms charge a flat fee and leave merchants to figure out growth on their own. We do the opposite — we put capital into our merchants, help them scale, and share only in the growth we helped create.
growthModel.takeaway: 32% of investor dollars flow directly INTO Tajikistan merchants in year one — making each dollar go 2–3× further than a spread-out multi-country expansion. Subscription is the floor; partnership revenue is the upside.

p1.title: We share growth, not revenue
p1.body: Merchants keep 100% of their existing revenue. We share only the incremental growth we helped create, for a fixed 24-month window.

p2.title: We invest first, they pay after
p2.body: Refresh funds the merchant's first marketing campaigns. If it doesn't work, they owe nothing. If it does, we share in the result.

p3.title: Aligned to the last dollar
p3.body: Our revenue scales with theirs. No hidden fees, no churn games, no upsell pressure — just one shared P&L direction.

capTable.title: How ownership evolves — base case AND bear case
capTable.subtitle: Showing both scenarios. Base case = plan hits Series A at $28M. Bear case = Series A at $20M with heavier ESOP top-ups. Even the bear case keeps founders at 25%+ at exit — long-term alignment holds.
capTable.footnote: Base case assumes Series A at 10–13× ARR (standard for emerging-markets SaaS with strong unit economics). Bear case assumes 7× ARR and 15% ESOP top-up each round. We showed both so you can underwrite the downside before the upside.

q1.q: Do I need to install anything?
q1.a: No. Refresh runs in the browser and mobile apps. Just sign up.

q2.a: Yes. The register keeps working offline and syncs when you're back online.

q3.q: How do I migrate from 1C or Excel?
q3.a: Import items, stock and customers via spreadsheets or API. We assist for free during onboarding.

q4.a: Base setup and training are free on all plans. Custom configuration is available on request.

q5.a: Yes — apps for owners and cashiers on iOS and Android.

q6.a: Russian, Tajik, Uzbek and English. Each employee can pick their own.
